name: Void Linux
description: Void Linux is an independent, rolling-release distribution built from scratch. It uses the runit init system and the XBPS package manager, and supports glibc and musl libc variants across many architectures.

name: Void Linux (musl)
description: Void Linux built against the musl C library instead of glibc, intended for users who want a smaller, statically-linkable runtime. Otherwise shares the same XBPS, runit and repository as the glibc build.

name: Void Linux (glibc)
description: Void Linux built against the GNU C Library (glibc). The default Void build, offering the broadest binary compatibility with mainstream Linux software while keeping the runit and XBPS stack.

name: NixOS
description: NixOS is an independently developed Linux distribution that uses the Nix package manager to build the entire system declaratively. It supports atomic upgrades, rollbacks, and reproducible system configurations from a single text file.

name: Guix System
description: Guix System (formerly GuixSD) is a transactional, stateless operating system built around the GNU Guix functional package manager. It lets users describe their entire system declaratively, supports per-user profiles, and offers a large repository of purely-functional package definitions.

name: Solus
description: Solus is an independently developed, rolling-release distribution built from scratch with the eopkg package manager. It is best known for shipping the Budgie desktop environment, designed in-house by the Solus team.

name: CRUX
description: CRUX is a lightweight, source-based, x86_64-optimized Linux distribution targeted at experienced users. It uses a simple ports system (similar to BSD ports) for building packages and ships without a graphical installer by default.

name: Exherbo
description: Exherbo is a source-based, rolling-release distribution aimed at developers and power users. It uses a unique exheres build format and the Paludis package manager, with a strong emphasis on best-practice packaging and reproducibility.

name: Exherbo (musl)
description: The musl libc variant of Exherbo, providing the same source-based workflow but built against the lightweight musl C library. Targets embedded and security-focused users who want a small, statically friendly runtime.

name: GoboLinux
description: GoboLinux is an independently developed distribution famous for its hierarchical, FHS-free file layout where every program lives in its own /Programs/<name>/<version> tree. The project wound down in 2023-2024 after two decades of development.

name: Chimera Linux
description: Chimera Linux is an independent, musl-based, source-built distribution using the dinit init system and a FreeBSD-ports-style build system (cports). It targets a clean, modern GNU userland with no systemd or GNU coreutils.

name: Bedrock Linux
description: Bedrock Linux is an independent meta-distribution that lets users mix and match components from other distros side by side. It manages heterogeneous package managers, init systems and libraries from a single unified system.

name: Clear Linux
description: Clear Linux was an Intel-optimized, rolling-release distribution built around stateless bundles and the swupd package manager. Intel announced the end of the project in 2025 after nearly a decade of development.

name: Tiny Core Linux
description: Tiny Core Linux is a minimal Linux distribution built around BusyBox, the FLTK toolkit and a tiny window manager. It runs entirely in RAM and assembles its desktop from on-demand extensions, with a base image of roughly 20 MB.

name: Puppy Linux
description: Puppy Linux is a family of small, lightweight Linux distributions designed to boot quickly and run comfortably from RAM on old or low-spec hardware. Most variants are built with the Woof-CE toolchain from upstream binary packages of other distros.

name: Fossapup
based_on: Puppy Linux (built from Ubuntu via Woof-CE)
description: Fossapup is the fully open-source branch of Puppy Linux, built with Woof-CE from Ubuntu LTS binary packages with all proprietary components removed. It targets users who want a small, FOSS-only Puppy experience.

name: Quirky Linux
description: Quirky is a Puppy-family distribution maintained by Barry Kauler and used as a test bed for new Puppy features such as EasyOS-style containers and the woofQ build system. It is the experimental sibling to mainstream Puppy releases.

name: EasyOS
description: EasyOS is a Puppy-family distribution built around a per-application container model where every program runs inside its own SFS or Easy Container. Designed for security isolation, easy rollback, and 'no dependency hell' installation of any application.

name: Fatdog64
description: Fatdog64 is a 64-bit-only member of the Puppy Linux family that targets modern PCs while keeping the lightweight, single-user feel of Puppy. It packages its own LFS-built GNU userland rather than reusing other distros' binaries.

name: Linux From Scratch
description: Linux From Scratch is a book project that walks users through building a complete Linux system from source, by hand. The 'distribution' is whatever the reader compiles; LFS provides the instructions and patch series, not a prebuilt image.

name: Beyond Linux From Scratch
description: Beyond Linux From Scratch continues the LFS book by guiding the reader through building a wider range of system and desktop software on top of a freshly built LFS base. It is the natural next step for users who want a fully customised desktop or server.

name: Cross-Compiled Linux From Scratch
description: Cross-Compiled Linux From Scratch extends the LFS book with instructions for building a complete Linux system that targets a different architecture than the build host. It covers toolchain construction, cross-compiling glibc, and producing a bootable target image.

name: Hardened Linux From Scratch
description: Hardened Linux From Scratch was an LFS-based book project that walked the reader through building a Linux system with additional security features such as stack-smashing protection, hardened toolchain flags, and PaX/grsecurity kernel patches.

name: OpenEmbedded
description: OpenEmbedded is a build framework that produces complete, custom Linux distributions for embedded devices. It provides the bitbake task engine and the OE-Core metadata, which other projects (such as Yocto) reuse to build images.

name: Ångström
description: Ångström is a Linux distribution for embedded devices built on top of OpenEmbedded. Originally derived from the Opie handhelds stack, it now provides reference images and feeds for ARM, MIPS and PowerPC hardware used in industrial and handheld devices.

name: Buildroot
description: Buildroot is a simple, efficient build system for producing complete embedded Linux systems from source. It produces a root filesystem image, a cross-compilation toolchain, a kernel and a bootloader, all configured through a single makefile and Kconfig interface.

name: Yocto Project
description: The Yocto Project is an open-source collaboration that provides templates, tools and methods for building custom Linux-based systems for embedded products. It uses the OpenEmbedded build system and the bitbake task engine to produce reproducible images.

name: Poky
description: Poky is the reference distribution of the Yocto Project: a minimal, board-independent build of the Yocto stack that demonstrates the build system and serves as a starting point for downstream embedded Linux distributions.

name: Lunar Linux
description: Lunar Linux is a source-based, rolling-release distribution where the 'lin' tool compiles everything from source using Sorcerer-derived recipes. It is heavily inspired by Sorcerer and aims to produce a clean, customisable Linux system from the user's own build decisions.

name: Sabotage Linux
description: Sabotage Linux is a source-based, musl-libc distribution built around the butch package manager and the s6 init system. The project went quiet after 2018 and the website is largely static, marking it as effectively discontinued.

name: 4MLinux
description: 4MLinux is a small, independent Linux distribution maintained by Zbigniew Konojacki that aims to deliver four core 'M' features: Maintenance, Multimedia, Miniserver, and Mystery. It builds a usable desktop, web server, and rescue environment from a single ~20 MB base.

name: SliTaz
description: SliTaz is a community-developed, ultra-lightweight Linux distribution that fits a working graphical environment into around 50 MB. It is built around BusyBox, the Openbox window manager and its own tazpkg package manager.

name: IPFire
description: IPFire is a hardened, independent Linux distribution designed for use as a firewall, router, proxy, or VPN gateway. It uses a modular Core update scheme and ships only the components required for a secure edge / network device.

name: Endian Firewall
description: Endian Firewall was an independent Linux-based firewall / UTM appliance distribution with a web management interface. Endian moved its focus to a commercial product line and the free community edition was effectively abandoned around 2020.

name: SmoothWall Express
description: SmoothWall Express was a free, open-source Linux firewall distribution built from a hardened custom kernel and managed entirely through a web interface. Development of the free Express edition was discontinued in 2020; the project continues as a commercial product.

name: IPCop
description: IPCop was a Linux firewall distribution forked from the early SmoothWall project and aimed at SOHO and small-business users. The project stopped producing releases after version 2.1.9 and is now considered discontinued, with users encouraged to move to IPFire.

name: Zeroshell
description: Zeroshell was a small Linux distribution for network services and appliances that ran entirely from a live CD / USB image. It provided a web-based interface for routing, VPN, QoS, captive portal, RADIUS and proxy functions; development has effectively stopped.

name: Wifiway
description: Wifiway was a Spanish-language live Linux distribution focused on wireless security auditing, packed with tools for Wi-Fi analysis and penetration testing. The project ended around 2012 and the website is no longer maintained.

name: Lucid Lynx / Lucide Linux
description: Lucide Linux (sometimes 'Lucid Lynx') was a small, independent, LXDE-focused Debian-style distribution intended as a lightweight daily-driver alternative to heavier GNOME-based systems. The project did not gain significant traction and is now considered discontinued.
